Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to dedicate about four hours a day to coursework, which sounds like a lot, but I spread it out because I'm a visual learner and need breaks to process things. I'd usually start by watching lecture videos and making diagrams or mind maps. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access materials online versus when I needed to work offline with my notes.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, like studying in the mornings when my head was clearer, but when deadlines piled up, all that went out the window. Some weeks I'd cram everything into a few intense days, other weeks I'd spread it out more evenly. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and how anxious I was feeling about upcoming assignments.
Q3: Usually a deadline was what got me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd also check in when I knew new video content was posted because those were really helpful for me as a visual learner. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements or if my peers had posted anything interesting in the discussions. But yeah, mostly it was deadline-driven.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me. I think it happened because I got into a rhythm with the five online courses I was taking and checking the LMS became part of my morning routine. I'd grab my coffee, log in, see what was new, and plan my day around it. The CBT sessions I did actually helped me establish better habits, which made the routine stick for a while.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me. Being a visual learner, I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I spent about 70 minutes on average watching videos, which might not sound like much, but I was taking detailed notes while watching. The discussion forums were also helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways than the instructor.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the longer reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text. With my visual learning style, those just didn't click for me the same way. I also minimally used some of the supplementary resources because honestly, with limited internet access sometimes, I had to prioritize what I downloaded and watched. Some optional quizzes I ignored because they felt like busywork.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could visualize the payoff, like understanding a concept that would show up on the test, I was all in. Activities that had clear, immediate relevance to my grades motivated me more than vague 'enrichment' stuff. Also, anything with good visual elements automatically felt more valuable to me.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt way less urgent, obviously. Required ones had this weight to them because I knew they affected my grade, so I prioritized those even when I wasn't feeling motivated. Optional stuff I'd only do if I had extra time or if I was genuinely curious about the topic. Sometimes I felt guilty skipping optional activities, but with five courses and limited resources, I had to make choices.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also made tons of handwritten diagrams and visual notes that I'd review constantly. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials, and I'd watch those instead. My physical notebook was probably my most-used study tool.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams from memory, testing myself with my own flashcards, or explaining concepts out loud to myself. I also studied with a couple of classmates in person sometimes, working through problems together. None of that shows up in any log, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy given my internet situation. I'd access a video or document once, download it if possible, and then return to it multiple times offline. Sometimes I'd watch a lecture video three or four times over different days, but the LMS would only show one view. I got pretty efficient at grabbing what I needed in one session and then working with it independently.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when all five of my courses had stuff due at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, not knowing where to start. The CBT sessions actually helped me work through some of that, which is probably why my mood ended up being pretty good by the end. But during those overwhelming moments, I'd sometimes just avoid the LMS entirely for a day or two.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in and just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities outside of school sometimes meant I'd rush through materials instead of engaging deeply. Confusion was a big one too—when I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes avoid it rather than face the frustration. My low resilience made bouncing back from setbacks harder than it probably should have been.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes and couldn't tell you a single thing that was said. Or I'd open a reading and just stare at it without processing the words. Those sessions felt like a waste, but sometimes I was too tired or anxious to do better. I'd have to go back and redo those sections later.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on assignments that seemed overwhelming or confusing. Even knowing they were important, I'd put them off because starting felt too hard. My assignment completion rate being around 67% reflects that—I'd sometimes run out of time because I'd delayed too long. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still a struggle. Fear of failure ironically made me more likely to fail.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be pretty quiet on the platform until a deadline approached, then suddenly I'd be logging in constantly, watching videos, checking forums for hints. My activity probably looked very spiky rather than steady. I know this isn't the ideal way to learn, but with low motivation and competing demands, deadlines were the main thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though sometimes not in the way you'd hope. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid the course for a bit. When I got my exam score back—55, which was just passing—I felt a mix of relief and disappointment. It pushed me to engage more with the remaining material, but also triggered some anxiety about whether I could do better.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on how I participated. When modules were clearly organized with visual roadmaps of what to do, I engaged more easily. But when things felt scattered or the expectations weren't clear, I'd get anxious and disengage. Having five online courses meant I appreciated structure even more because I needed to efficiently manage my time across all of them.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my visual learning style. Clear deadlines and organized modules helped too. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content or when the discussion forums felt forced rather than natural. Also, some weeks the workload spiked dramatically, which made it hard to keep up and stay engaged across all my courses.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for something specific but can't find it, so they keep clicking around. Or maybe they're confused and revisiting materials trying to understand, but never quite getting there enough to complete the assignment. It could also be anxiety—logging in repeatedly to check things but being too stressed to actually produce work. I've definitely experienced versions of all of these.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me in some ways. I passed the course despite relatively low LMS engagement because a lot of my real learning happened offline. If someone is a good self-studier or has access to alternative resources, they might not need to be constantly logged in. Also, quality of engagement matters more than quantity—one focused hour beats three distracted ones.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, and what resources you click on. That's useful for seeing general trends. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They miss offline studying, peer discussions, the emotional state you're in while working. They show behavior but not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling mentally present while studying. It's when I'm curious about the material, not just trying to get through it. For me, real engagement is when I'm actively making connections, creating visual representations, and thinking about how concepts relate to each other. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's genuinely wrestling with ideas.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with my handwritten notes and diagrams, or the anxiety I pushed through to keep studying. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions or the YouTube videos that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show how the CBT sessions improved my overall approach to the course, or how my mood shifted from anxious to actually feeling okay about my progress. The human side of learning is invisible to the data.
